agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"Smart Route Optimization is a logistics feature that automatically plans and dynamically adjusts delivery routes for drivers during their shifts. It spans multiple bounded contexts: route planning, delivery lifecycle, fleet/driver management, and customer notifications. The feature encodes real-time operational logistics where route state is mutable and reactive to field events.","bounded_context":"This feature touches at minimum four bounded contexts: (1) Route Planning — owns route calculation, optimization, and recalculation triggers; (2) Delivery Management — owns delivery lifecycle (pending, in-progress, failed, completed) and zone assignment; (3) Fleet Management — owns driver profiles, shift lifecycle, vehicle classes, and licensing constraints; (4) Customer Notification — owns notification triggers, delivery windows, and customer-facing ETAs. Each context has its own language and must communicate via well-defined integration events, not shared state.","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Shift","definition":"A bounded work period for a driver. Begins explicitly (driver starts shift) and ends explicitly. A shift is the lifecycle anchor for route assignment — no shift, no route."},{"term":"Zone","definition":"A geographic partition used to scope deliveries and driver assignment. A zone is an operational boundary, not just a geo-fence — it carries zone-specific configuration (e.g., stop time overrides)."},{"term":"Optimized Route","definition":"An ordered sequence of delivery stops calculated by the system to minimize total travel time/distance for a driver within their zone for the current shift."},{"term":"Pending Delivery","definition":"A delivery that has been created and assigned to a zone but has not yet been assigned to a driver's route or completed/failed. This is the pool from which routes are built."},{"term":"Failed Delivery","definition":"A delivery where the driver was unable to complete the handoff (customer unavailable, access denied, etc.). Triggers route recalculation because the stop is removed from the active sequence."},{"term":"Route Deviation","definition":"When a driver's actual position or stop sequence diverges from the system-suggested route beyond a defined threshold. This is a driver-initiated event, not a system error."},{"term":"Next 3 Stops Window","definition":"A rolling window of the three upcoming stops on the driver's active route. Entering this window triggers customer notification. The window advances as stops are completed."},{"term":"Licensed Vehicle Class","definition":"A classification of the vehicle a driver is authorized to operate (e.g., motorcycle, van, truck, freight). This constrains which deliveries can be assigned — it is a hard physical and legal constraint."},{"term":"Delivery Vehicle Class Requirement","definition":"Each delivery/shipment has a minimum vehicle class requirement based on size, weight, or handling needs (e.g., a freight shipment requires a freight-class vehicle)."},{"term":"Stop Time","definition":"The estimated duration a driver spends at each delivery stop. Defaults to 4 minutes but is overridable per zone by the operations team. Used in ETA calculations and route optimization."},{"term":"Recalculation Trigger","definition":"An event that invalidates the current optimized route and forces recomputation. Three defined triggers: new delivery added to zone, delivery marked failed, driver deviation."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"A driver must have an active shift before any route can be assigned to them.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"A route without an active shift has no lifecycle owner. Assigning deliveries to an off-duty driver corrupts operational state — the driver is not working, cannot fulfill stops, and the deliveries would be stranded.","testable_assertion":"Given a driver with no active shift, when the system attempts to assign a route, then assignment MUST be rejected."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"A driver can only be assigned deliveries whose vehicle class requirement is at or below their licensed vehicle class.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"This is a legal and physical constraint. A motorcycle cannot carry freight. Violating this means deliveries physically cannot be fulfilled and may violate transportation regulations. No business policy can override physics or law.","testable_assertion":"Given a driver licensed for motorcycle and a delivery requiring freight vehicle class, when route assignment is attempted, then that delivery MUST be excluded from the driver's route."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"The route must be recalculated when a new pending delivery is added to the driver's active zone.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"While operationally important, the business could decide to batch recalculations (e.g., every 5 minutes) or defer until the driver completes their next stop. The trigger policy is tunable.","testable_assertion":"Given a driver with an active route in Zone A, when a new delivery is created in Zone A, then the driver's route MUST be recalculated to include the new delivery."},{"id":"BR-4","rule":"The route must be recalculated when a delivery on the active route is marked as failed.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"The recalculation itself is policy — the system could simply remove the failed stop and keep the remaining order. Recalculation is the preferred policy but not a state integrity requirement.","testable_assertion":"Given a driver with stops [A, B, C, D] and delivery B is marked failed, when the failure is recorded, then the route MUST be recalculated with B removed from the stop pool."},{"id":"BR-5","rule":"The route must be recalculated when the driver manually deviates from the suggested route.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"Deviation-triggered recalculation is a UX/efficiency policy. The system could also simply flag the deviation and let the driver continue. This is a product decision, not a data integrity rule.","testable_assertion":"Given a driver deviates from the suggested route sequence, when deviation is detected, then the route MUST be recalculated from the driver's current position with remaining undelivered stops."},{"id":"BR-6","rule":"Customers must be notified when their delivery enters the next 3 stops window on the driver's active route.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"The notification window size (3 stops) is a product/UX decision that could be changed to 2 or 5 stops. The notification itself is a policy, not a data integrity constraint.","testable_assertion":"Given a driver completes a stop, when the remaining route is recalculated/advanced and delivery X is now within the next 3 stops, then the customer for delivery X MUST receive a notification."},{"id":"BR-7","rule":"A customer must receive the notification at most once per delivery per route window entry — no duplicate notifications for the same delivery.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Sending duplicate notifications degrades customer trust and creates confusion. Idempotency of notifications is a data integrity concern — the system must track notification state per delivery.","testable_assertion":"Given a customer was already notified that their delivery is in the next 3 stops window, when a route recalculation occurs and the same delivery is still in the next 3 stops, then no additional notification MUST be sent."},{"id":"BR-8","rule":"The default estimated stop time is 4 minutes per delivery stop.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is an operational parameter, explicitly described as overridable by the operations team. It is a configuration value, not a hard constraint.","testable_assertion":"Given no zone-specific override exists, when ETA is calculated for a route, then each stop MUST use 4 minutes as the estimated stop duration."},{"id":"BR-9","rule":"The operations team can override the estimated stop time per zone.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is an administrative capability, a business policy lever. The override mechanism is a feature, not a system invariant.","testable_assertion":"Given the operations team sets stop time for Zone A to 6 minutes, when a route in Zone A is calculated, then each stop MUST use 6 minutes as the estimated stop duration."},{"id":"BR-10","rule":"A route only contains deliveries from the driver's assigned zone.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Zone boundaries are operational partitions. Cross-zone assignment would violate the zone-based dispatch model and could result in conflicting assignments (two drivers assigned the same delivery from different zone views). This is a data consistency constraint.","testable_assertion":"Given a driver assigned to Zone A, when their route is calculated, then all deliveries on the route MUST belong to Zone A and no delivery from Zone B or any other zone MUST be included."}],"acceptance_criteria":[{"id":"AC-1","given":"A driver starts their shift in Zone A with 10 pending deliveries in Zone A","when":"The shift is activated","then":"The system assigns an optimized route containing all 10 pending deliveries that satisfy the driver's vehicle class, ordered for optimal travel efficiency"},{"id":"AC-2","given":"A driver has an active route in Zone A with 8 remaining stops","when":"A new delivery is added to Zone A","then":"The route is recalculated to include the new delivery in the optimal position, and the driver sees the updated route"},{"id":"AC-3","given":"A driver has an active route [A, B, C, D, E]","when":"Delivery C is marked as failed","then":"The route is recalculated with stops [A, B, D, E] reoptimized, and C is removed from the active route"},{"id":"AC-4","given":"A driver has an active route [A, B, C, D, E] and is heading to stop A","when":"The driver skips A and drives toward C","then":"Route deviation is detected and the route is recalculated from the driver's current position with all undelivered stops"},{"id":"AC-5","given":"A driver completes stop 5 of 12, and deliveries at stops 6, 7, and 8 have not yet been notified","when":"The route advances","then":"Customers for stops 6, 7, and 8 receive a notification that their delivery is approaching"},{"id":"AC-6","given":"A customer was already notified their delivery is in the next 3 stops window","when":"A route recalculation occurs and their delivery remains in the next 3 stops","then":"No duplicate notification is sent to that customer"},{"id":"AC-7","given":"A motorcycle-licensed driver starts their shift in a zone with both small parcel and freight deliveries","when":"The route is calculated","then":"Only small parcel deliveries are included; freight deliveries are excluded from the route"},{"id":"AC-8","given":"The operations team has set Zone B stop time to 6 minutes","when":"A route is calculated for a driver in Zone B with 5 stops","then":"The ETA calculation uses 6 minutes per stop (30 minutes of stop time) plus travel time"},{"id":"AC-9","given":"No zone-specific stop time override exists for Zone C","when":"A route is calculated for a driver in Zone C with 5 stops","then":"The ETA calculation uses the default 4 minutes per stop (20 minutes of stop time) plus travel time"},{"id":"AC-10","given":"A driver in Zone A has an active route","when":"A new delivery is created in Zone B","then":"The driver's route is NOT recalculated and the Zone B delivery is NOT added to their route"}],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"Route recalculation cascade: a new delivery triggers recalculation, which changes the next 3 stops window, which triggers new notifications, and during recalculation another delivery is added","expected_behavior":"Recalculations must be serialized or debounced per driver to prevent race conditions. Each recalculation must use the latest state. Notification checks happen after recalculation settles, not during."},{"scenario":"All pending deliveries in a zone exceed the driver's vehicle class","expected_behavior":"The driver starts their shift but receives an empty route. The system should surface this to dispatch/operations as an alert — a driver is on shift with no assignable work."},{"scenario":"Driver is the only driver in a zone and their shift ends with undelivered stops","expected_behavior":"Undelivered stops return to the pending pool for the zone. They are not orphaned. The system should alert operations that deliveries are unassigned."},{"scenario":"A delivery is marked failed and then a retry is requested before recalculation completes","expected_behavior":"The retry creates a new pending delivery (or reactivates the original). The recalculation must pick up the current state — if the delivery is pending again, it should be included in the new route."},{"scenario":"Driver deviates but then returns to the original route before recalculation completes","expected_behavior":"If deviation detection has a threshold (time or distance), minor deviations that self-correct should not trigger recalculation. The spec does not define a deviation threshold — this needs clarification."},{"scenario":"Two drivers in the same zone — how are pending deliveries partitioned?","expected_behavior":"The spec does not address multi-driver zones. Are deliveries split between drivers? Does each driver get a subset? Is there a partitioning algorithm? This is a critical gap."},{"scenario":"Customer notification sent but then route recalculation moves their delivery outside the next 3 stops window","expected_behavior":"The notification has already been sent and cannot be un-sent. The customer may receive a misleading ETA. The system should send an updated notification if the delivery moves out of the window after notification."},{"scenario":"Stop time override is changed mid-shift while drivers have active routes","expected_behavior":"Should active routes be recalculated with the new stop time, or only apply to new route calculations? The spec does not clarify. Recommendation: apply on next recalculation, not force an immediate one."},{"scenario":"A delivery's vehicle class requirement is ambiguous or missing","expected_behavior":"Deliveries without a vehicle class requirement must default to the lowest class (assignable to any driver) to avoid stranding deliveries. This should be validated at delivery creation time."},{"scenario":"Driver starts shift but there are zero pending deliveries in their zone","expected_behavior":"Driver receives an empty route. System should monitor for new deliveries and assign/recalculate when one appears. Driver is not in an error state — they are idle and available."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"Route optimization directly reduces fuel costs, driver overtime, and increases deliveries per shift. Incorrect vehicle class assignment could result in failed deliveries, return trips, and wasted driver hours. Accurate ETAs reduce missed deliveries and re-delivery costs.","operational":"Real-time recalculation increases dispatch complexity and system load. Each recalculation trigger is a computation event — at scale with many drivers and zones, this requires careful capacity planning. The 4-minute default stop time is a critical calibration — too low causes late deliveries, too high causes underutilization.","user":"Customers receive proactive notifications improving delivery experience and reducing 'where is my package' support calls. Drivers get optimized routes reducing cognitive load and windshield time. Operations team gets zone-level control over stop time estimates.","risk":"Route recalculation race conditions could cause drivers to receive conflicting instructions. Vehicle class enforcement failure is a legal/safety risk. Notification duplication erodes customer trust. Multi-driver zone partitioning gap could cause duplicate assignments (two drivers assigned the same delivery).","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Drivers","impact":"Receive optimized routes reducing decision fatigue. Must trust the system — frequent unnecessary recalculations or poor optimization erodes trust and causes drivers to ignore suggestions."},{"group":"Customers","impact":"Receive proactive delivery notifications. Inaccurate ETAs or duplicate notifications degrade experience. Notification when delivery moves OUT of window is unaddressed."},{"group":"Operations Team","impact":"Gains zone-level stop time configuration. Needs visibility into empty routes, unassigned deliveries, and vehicle class mismatches. Likely needs a dashboard for route health."},{"group":"Dispatch / Fleet Management","impact":"Must ensure vehicle class data is accurate and current. Driver-zone assignment becomes critical. Multi-driver zone partitioning logic is undefined."},{"group":"Engineering / Platform","impact":"Real-time recalculation at scale is computationally expensive. Needs debouncing, serialization, and eventual consistency strategy. Deviation detection requires GPS integration and threshold definition."}]},"confidence":{"level":52,"reasoning":"The spec is clear on single-driver-per-zone happy path but has significant gaps around multi-driver zones, deviation thresholds, notification lifecycle (what happens when delivery leaves the window after notification), and recalculation concurrency. These are not minor details — they are core operational scenarios that will occur daily in production. The vehicle class constraint and stop time override are well-specified.","high_confidence_areas":["Vehicle class constraint is a hard invariant — clear, unambiguous, legally grounded","Stop time default and override mechanism is well-defined","Single-driver route lifecycle (start shift → assign route → recalculate on triggers) is clear","Customer notification trigger (next 3 stops) is clear in the happy path"],"low_confidence_areas":["Multi-driver zone behavior is completely unspecified — this is critical for any real deployment","Route deviation detection threshold is undefined — what constitutes a deviation?","Notification lifecycle when delivery moves out of window after notification is sent","Recalculation concurrency and ordering guarantees are unspecified","Whether recalculation is synchronous (driver waits) or asynchronous (driver continues on old route)","How zone assignment for drivers works — is it static or dynamic?"],"assumptions":["Each zone has exactly one driver at a time (since multi-driver is unspecified)","Vehicle class is a strict hierarchy (motorcycle < van < truck < freight) not a tag system","Route deviation is detected via GPS position diverging from expected path","Recalculation replaces the entire remaining route, not just inserts/removes a single stop","The 'next 3 stops' window is based on route sequence position, not geographic proximity"]},"escalations":[{"type":"ambiguity","description":"Multi-driver zone partitioning: The spec assumes a single driver per zone but real logistics operations frequently have multiple drivers in the same zone. How should pending deliveries be partitioned among multiple drivers? Options: (A) each driver gets a geographically clustered subset, (B) deliveries are assigned first-come-first-served, (C) a dispatcher manually partitions, (D) zones are always 1:1 with drivers. This fundamentally changes the route optimization algorithm.","affected_stakeholders":["Drivers","Dispatch","Operations Team","Engineering"],"options":["A: Algorithmic partitioning by geographic clustering","B: First-come-first-served assignment","C: Manual dispatcher partitioning","D: Enforce 1:1 zone-to-driver mapping"],"recommendation":"Clarify with product. If multi-driver zones are in scope, this needs its own design. If zones are 1:1, make that an explicit invariant."},{"type":"ambiguity","description":"Route deviation threshold: What constitutes a 'manual deviation'? Is it skipping a stop, driving in the wrong direction for N meters, being off-route for N minutes, or the driver explicitly pressing a 'skip' button? Without a threshold, deviation detection is either too sensitive (constant recalculations from GPS noise) or too lax (driver is 20 minutes off-route before recalculation).","affected_stakeholders":["Drivers","Engineering"],"options":["A: Sequence-based — driver completes stops out of order","B: GPS-based — driver is more than X meters from expected path for Y seconds","C: Explicit — driver presses a 'deviate' or 'skip' button","D: Hybrid — sequence and GPS combined"],"recommendation":"Start with sequence-based detection (driver completes stops out of suggested order) as it is deterministic and does not require GPS threshold tuning. Add GPS-based detection as a Phase 2 enhancement."},{"type":"ambiguity","description":"Notification lifecycle on route changes: If a customer is notified their delivery is in the next 3 stops, but a recalculation then moves it to stop 7, the customer has a stale expectation. Should the system send a 'delayed' notification? The spec is silent on this.","affected_stakeholders":["Customers","Operations Team"],"options":["A: Send an updated 'your delivery has been delayed' notification","B: Do nothing — the original notification is informational only","C: Only send the initial notification when the delivery is truly 'locked in' to the next 3 stops (no more recalculations expected)"],"recommendation":"Option A — send a corrective notification. Customer trust requires accurate expectations. This should be a separate acceptance criterion."},{"type":"missing_context","description":"Route optimization algorithm criteria: The spec says 'optimized route' but does not define optimization criteria. Is it shortest distance, shortest time, fewest left turns, delivery priority, customer SLA windows, or a weighted combination? This fundamentally changes what 'correct' means for route output.","affected_stakeholders":["Drivers","Customers","Operations Team","Engineering"],"options":["A: Shortest total travel distance","B: Shortest total time (including stop times and traffic)","C: Priority-weighted (some deliveries are more urgent)","D: SLA-driven (deliveries with time windows first)"],"recommendation":"Clarify with product. At minimum, define whether delivery time windows/SLAs exist. If they do, optimization must respect them as hard constraints, not just minimize distance."}],"rejection_reasons":[]}
agent_output_excerpt: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"Smart Route Optimization is a logistics feature that automatically plans and dynamically adjusts delivery routes for drivers during their shifts. It spans multiple bounded contexts: route planning, delivery lifecycle, fleet/driver management, and customer notifications. The feature encodes real-time operational logistics where route state is mutable and reactive to field events.","bounded_context":"This feature touches at minimum four bounded contexts: (1)
